coredata.dc:title: Perylene tetracarboxylic acid-folate conjugated carbon quantum dots for targeted photodynamic therapy of prostate cancer

coredata.dc:description: This study reports the facile one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of water-soluble perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) conjugated carbon quantum dots (Per CDs) and their folate conjugate (PerFACDs), addressing the insolubility of PTCDA, a promising photosensitizer. Both CDs were thoroughly characterized using HRTEM, FTIR, Raman, and XPS techniques. Their singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ), zeta potential, absorption and photoluminescence spectra were also studied. Their anti-human prostate cancer activity was tested using PC-3 cells as a model. The mode of cell death was investigated using flow cytometry, and their heamolytic activity and effect on the viability of normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells and were evaluated to assess the safety. The produced CDs were uniform spheres with diameters below 8 nm. At physiological pH, PerCDs exhibited a surface charge of −9 mV, while PerFACDs had −12 mV and a ΦΔ of 0.22. The biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the produced CDs were evaluated in vitro normal human fibroblast (HFF-1) cells, and peripheral blood cells. Both PerCDs and PerFACD showed negligible toxicity toward normal cells. To assess selective cytotoxicity, the selectivity index (SI) was calculated under dark conditions, yielding values of 1.47 for PerCDs and 2.4 for PerFACD, indicating significantly enhanced selectivity of PerFACD toward PC3 cells. Upon illumination using monochromatic 520 nm light-emitting diode (1.5 mW/cm2), a total eradication of PC-3 cells was achieved within 60 min of irradiation using Per FA CD that was significantly more potent than Per CDs. Per FA CDs-PDT induced an apoptotic cell death mode at shorter irradiation times and a necrotic one at longer durations. These findings suggest that our synthesized third generation photosensitizer Per CDs and Per FA CD hold promise as safe and effective candidates for targeted PDT of prostate cancer.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images for Per CDs at different magnifications and scales at (A) 20 nm, (B) 10 nm, and (C) 5 nm, while HRTEM images for Per FACDs at different magnifications and scales at (F) 20 nm, (G) 10 nm, and (H) 5 nm. The calculated d spacing for Per CDs, and Per FACDs at (D), and (i), respectively. (E), and (J) represent particle size histogram for Per CDs, and Per FACDs, respectively.
(A) FTIR analysis for PTCDA, PerCD, PerFACD, (B) Zeta potential of Per CD, PerFACD at pH 4, and pH 5. (C), and (D) represents the Raman analysis.
XPS analysis of Per CDs, and Per FACDs.

(A-D) presents 2D fluorescence topographical maps of PerFACDs and Per CDs, revealing dual emission characteristics in their photoluminescence contour plots. An excitation-independent emission at longer wavelengths (∼500–600 nm) suggests a stable, carbonized core with π-conjugated domains formed during heat condensation, consistent with carbon dot core formation [82]. Conversely, excitation-dependent emission at shorter wavelengths (∼400–480 nm) indicates surface-bound or partially oxidized functional groups (e.g., carboxyl or hydroxyl) responsible for fluorescence variability. This observation supports folic acid conjugation to carbon quantum dots, leading to distinct optical transitions [83].

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra, (B) Fluorescence spectra for the raw PTCDA, and synthesized carbon quantum dots PerCD, and PerFACD at excitation wavelength 480 nm, (C) Fluorescence spectra for PerCD at different excitation wavelengths (480–510 nm), (D) Fluorescence spectra for PerFACD at different excitation wavelengths (480–510 nm).

(A) Hemolysis rate %, of RBCs from mouse treated by the synthesized CDs (B) the Photographs of hemolytic activity assay, and (C) cytotoxicity test on normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells with different concentrations of Per CDs, and PerFACD, (D) cytotoxicity test on human skin fibroblast cells (HFF1) with different concentrations of Per CDs, and PerFACD. The data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). Error bars in all relevant figures show the standard deviation (SD), and statistical significance of differences between Per CDs and PerFACDs samples is denoted by asterisks and p-value thresholds. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.001 were considered statistically significant.

MTT assay to assess PDT cytotoxicity of PC-3 cells with different concentrations of PerCDs, and PerFACD, and with irradiation time 30 min, and 60 min. The irradiation was performed under 520 nm and 1.5 W cm−2. The data are shown as mean ± SD (n = 3). Analysis of variance (ANOVA) showed the differences among the concentrations were significant in relation to the control group (0 concentration of carbon quantum dots) and dark and PDT group (* p < 0.05).

Flow cytometry analysis of Per FACD on the apoptosis and cell cycle progression. PC-3 cells were cultured with and without Per FACD (12.5 μg/mL) for 48 h, in dark and light conditions. Apoptotic cell death was measured by flow cytometry after staining with annexin V-FITC and PI.
illustrates the synthesis of Per CD, and Per FACD through solvothermal reaction at 180 °C, for 6 h.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports the facile one-pot hydrothermal synthesis of water-soluble perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) conjugated carbon quantum dots (Per CDs) and their folate conjugate (PerFACDs), addressing the insolubility of PTCDA, a promising photosensitizer. Both CDs were thoroughly characterized using HRTEM, FTIR, Raman, and XPS techniques. Their singlet oxygen quantum yield (ΦΔ), zeta potential, absorption and photoluminescence spectra were also studied. Their anti-human prostate cancer activity was tested using PC-3 cells as a model. The mode of cell death was investigated using flow cytometry, and their heamolytic activity and effect on the viability of normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells and were evaluated to assess the safety. The produced CDs were uniform spheres with diameters below 8 nm. At physiological pH, PerCDs exhibited a surface charge of −9 mV, while PerFACDs had −12 mV and a ΦΔ of 0.22. The biocompatibility and cytotoxicity of the produced CDs were evaluated in vitro normal human fibroblast (HFF-1) cells, and peripheral blood cells. Both PerCDs and PerFACD showed negligible toxicity toward normal cells. To assess selective cytotoxicity, the selectivity index (SI) was calculated under dark conditions, yielding values of 1.47 for PerCDs and 2.4 for PerFACD, indicating significantly enhanced selectivity of PerFACD toward PC3 cells. Upon illumination using monochromatic 520 nm light-emitting diode (1.5 mW/cm2), a total eradication of PC-3 cells was achieved within 60 min of irradiation using Per FA CD that was significantly more potent than Per CDs. Per FA CDs-PDT induced an apoptotic cell death mode at shorter irradiation times and a necrotic one at longer durations. These findings suggest that our synthesized third generation photosensitizer Per CDs and Per FA CD hold promise as safe and effective candidates for targeted PDT of prostate cancer.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Photodynamic therapy (PDT) holds significant promise in cancer treatment [1,2]. During PDT, photosensitizers (PSs) are activated by light to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide radicals (O2 •−), hydroxide radicals (•OH), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Type-I), and singlet oxygen (1O2) (Type-II) [3,4]. Singlet oxygen (1O2), a key cytotoxic agent produced by PSs, disrupts tumor blood vessels, induces oxidative stress in cancer cells, and impairs cellular functions [5,6]. To enhance PDT efficacy, researchers aim to improve the precision of cancer cell targeting and increase the depth of light penetration [7,8]. By selectively delivering PSs to tumors, PDT minimizes systemic toxicity and reduces damage to healthy tissues [9]. Traditional PSs are often hydrophobic organic compounds that tend to aggregate in biological environments, limiting their tumor accumulation and therapeutic efficacy [10,11].

Perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic acid derivatives, referred to as PTCDA, generated from perylene dyes, have demonstrated efficacy in the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). As known, the perylene derivatives exhibited exceptionally high luminescence quantum yields [12]. Nonetheless, one undesirable characteristic of perylene complexes is the formation of molecular aggregates, which is promoted by the planar structure of the perylene molecules. Perylene dyes are recognized for their limited solubility in organic solvents, typically ranging from 1 to 2 mg L−1 [13]. The aggregation of PTCDA in aqueous media, driven by strong intermolecular π–π interactions, necessitates the development of water-soluble PTCDA for optimal bioimaging and sensing applications [14].

The current methods for preparing water-soluble PTCDA necessitate complex, multi-step organic synthesis, often involving the use of toxic organic solvents [15]. Consequently, developing a straightforward, cost-effective, and environmentally friendly method for the direct synthesis of water-soluble PTCDA presents a significant challenge [16].

The synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CDs) derived from photosensitizers to address solubility challenges in photodynamic therapy (PDT) represents an intriguing field of study and aims to improve the solubility, biocompatibility, and stability of photosensitizers while preserving their photodynamic efficacy [17]. Carbon quantum dots represent a highly promising category of nanomaterials, demonstrating significant potential for PDT applications. This is primarily attributed to their remarkable water solubility, low toxicity, ease of functionalization, and favorable photophysical properties [18].

CDs, as a new class of photosensitizers, offer several advantages. These nanomaterials, typically less than 10 nm in diameter [19], exhibit low toxicity, tunable optical properties, and excellent biocompatibility [20,21]. CD synthesis primarily utilizes the bottom-up approach, enabling large-scale production from sustainable sources [22,23]. By carefully controlling synthesis conditions, researchers can tailor CD properties for specific applications [24]. CDs can function as both PSs and drug delivery vehicles, enhancing PDT outcomes by directly generating ROS or by improving the delivery and activation of other PSs [25].

Prostate cancer (PCa) ranks among the most prevalent cancers in men, contributing to considerable morbidity and mortality globally [26]. Despite advancements in treatment modalities including surgery, radiation therapy, and chemotherapy, disease recurrence is common, and metastatic prostate cancer continues to pose significant treatment challenges [27]. With advancements in the understanding of prostate cancer biology, there is an increasing focus on the development of targeted therapies that selectively address cancer cells while reducing damage to healthy tissues [28]. A promising approach for targeted therapy in prostate cancer is folate receptor (FR)-targeted therapy. Folate receptors, particularly the FR-α isoform, exhibit overexpression in multiple cancer types, including prostate cancer [29]. The receptors enable the cellular uptake of folate and folate-conjugated agents through receptor-mediated endocytosis [30]. Researchers have developed therapies that utilize folate as a targeting ligand to selectively deliver cytotoxic drugs, imaging agents, and photosensitizers to prostate cancer cells, capitalizing on the overexpression of folate receptors [31]. This targeted approach enhances treatment specificity and efficacy while reducing the systemic toxicity commonly linked to conventional therapies [32]. Folate-conjugated nanoparticles and photosensitizers have emerged as a promising approach for the treatment of prostate cancer, particularly through photodynamic therapy (PDT) [33]. The targeted delivery of photosensitizers via folate receptors increases their accumulation in prostate cancer cells, thereby improving the selectivity and efficacy of photodynamic therapy (PDT) [34].

For the first time, this study presents the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CDs) with integrated perylene tetracarboxylic anhydride (PTCDA) photosensitizers, termed Per CDs, and their folate-conjugated analogs (PerFACDs). Following thorough characterization and investigation of their photochemical properties, we examined their efficiency in mediating photodynamic therapy against the PC-3 prostate cancer cell line and elucidated the cell death mechanism.

Perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), folic acid, coumarin (≥99 % (HPLC), 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT powder), Dulbecco's Phosphate-Buffered Saline (DPBS), DMEM, and Triton X-100 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (USA). Human prostate cancer cell line PC-3, and Human skin fibroblast cell lines HFF-1 was obtained from the National Research Center, Dokki, Egypt. Fetal bovine serum and sterile cell culture Pencillin/Strepotomycin mixture were purchased from BioWest, France. Aptoptosis/Necrosis detection kit (BD Pharmingen™ FITC Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit I) was procured from BD Biosciences, Germany. All the experiments used ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of perylene derivatives carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of 0.1 g PTCDA and 0.1 g folic acid was prepared in a 150 mL Teflon autoclave with 20 mL deionized water. This mixture was then subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h. After centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min, the supernatant (dark brown solution) was diluted three-fold with ultrapure water. Subsequent purification was achieved through dialysis against ultrapure water using a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h. To ensure efficient removal of small molecules, the dialysis water was refreshed every half hour during the initial three hours. Finally, the purified solution within the dialysis bag was freeze-dried to obtain dark solid folate conjugated CDs (PerFACDs). The same protocol was applied without adding folic acid to produce the unconjugated Per CDs.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was conducted with a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope to examine the dimensions and morphology of the scattered carbon dots. The measurement of particle size distribution for Per CDs and Per FA CDs was conducted through dynamic light scattering (DLS) utilizing Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK) at 25 °C and pH 5 and 7. Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra was acquired using an IR Affinity-1 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Solid-state Raman spectra were obtained at ambient temperature using a SENTERRA instrument from BRUKER, Germany. A 785 nm wavelength laser was utilized with 50× magnification and an integration time of 2.03 s. UV–Vis absorption measurements were performed with a UV-2350 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence properties were investigated using an RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron analyzer (K-alpha+, ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) was used to get X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of Per CDs and Per FA CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) of the PerCD, and PerACD were determined using the comparative method described by Williams [36], which involves comparison with a standard compound of known quantum yield. In this study, the water-soluble N,N′-di(2-(trimethylammoniumiodide)ethylene) perylenediimide (TAIPDI; 0.05 mM in water) dye was used as a standard material with a quantum yield of 0.028 [35]. The fluorescence quantum yields of the examined compounds were calculated using the following equation:

Where Φ sample represents the quantum yield of the sample, Φ Standard represents the fluorescence quantum yield of the standard TAIPDI in water. Asample and Astandard represent the absorbance of the sample and the standard at the excitation wavelength (375 nm), respectively. Fsample and Astandard are the integrated fluorescence intensity of the sample and standard, respectively. n is the refractive index of the water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of singlet oxygen quantum yields
ce:section.ce:para: Singlet oxygen quantum yields were directly determined by measuring singlet oxygen phosphorescence (emission wavelength ∼ 1275 nm) after photoexcitation of the compounds in acetonitrile at ambient temperature. Excitation was performed using the third harmonic of a Q-switched Nd:YAG laser (355 nm, ∼8 ns pulse duration, pulse energy ≤20 mJ), specifically the LP980 system from Edinburgh Instruments. The 1275 nm phosphorescence signal of singlet oxygen (1O2) was detected using a Hamamatsu H10330–45 near-infrared (NIR) detector. Optical densities of the sample and standard were matched at 355 nm [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hemolysis testing of synthesized carbon quantum dots (Per CDs and Per FA CDs)
ce:section.ce:para:
For this experiment, 500 μL of fresh mouse blood was collected with a minimal amount of anticoagulant. The blood sample was centrifuged at 2000 rpm for 5 min to remove the supernatant, after which the red blood cells were resuspended. The red blood cells were then sedimented in 1 mL of normal saline and centrifuged again at 2000 rpm for 5 min to remove the supernatant. This procedure was repeated three to four times until the supernatant became nearly colorless. Once this was achieved, the red blood cells were resuspended in 1 mL of normal saline. Concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30, 40, and 50 μg/mL were prepared by taking 100 μL of the red blood cell suspension and adding the synthesized carbon quantum dots (per CD and per FA CD). The final solution volume was 2 mL, with the negative control consisting of red blood cells resuspended in normal saline, and the positive control consisting of red blood cells resuspended in 0.5 % (wt) Triton X − 100. The tubes were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h, followed by centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 5 min to facilitate image capture. The supernatant was then measured at 450 nm using a spectrophotometer [37]. The hemolysis rate was calculated using the following formula:
Hemolysis Rate % =
A T
−
A Neg
A Pos
−
A Neg
× 100
At the absorbance of the test sample, A neg and Apos represent negative and positive control, respectively.

The cytotoxicity of the synthesized nanoparticles was evaluated against normal human fibroblast (HFF-1) cells, peripheral blood cells as normal cell lines, and the PC-3 cell lines using the Thiazolyl Blue Tetrazolium Bromide (MTT) method [38–40]. Concisely, 96-well plates containing 10 × 103 cells of the PC3 cell lines were incubated overnight at 37 °C, 5 % CO2, and 90 % humidity. The final concentrations of the nanoparticles were 0, 6.25,12. 5, 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL. To assess the intrinsic toxicity, cells were incubated in the darkness for 48 h before measuring the cell viability. To assess PDT response, cells were irradiated for different time intervals (30 and 60 min) using a monochromatic light emitting diode at 520 nm (1.5 mW/cm2) at the end of the incubation period after nanoparticle removal. A light control was also included in which cells were incubated with nanoparticle-free medium and irradiation for 60 min. MTT assay was conducted as described earlier [41,42]. The optical density of the solubilized formazan crystals was measured at a wavelength of 570 nm. The percentage of cell viability in each well was determined by applying the following formula: The relative cell viability (%) = [X/Y] × 100. Where X is the mean optical density of the tested sample and Y is the mean optical density of the reference (control) sample. The samples underwent triplicate testing and were compared to untreated cells as a negative reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of the mode of cell death using flow cytometry
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the obtained results of the MTT assay, PC-3 cells were treated with 12.5 μg/mL of Per FA CDs for 48 h. Irradiations were either 30 or 60 min. The treated cells were collected and stained with Annexin V FITC and PI provided by the kit (BD Pharmingen™ FITC Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit I). Light control and dark control groups were included. The assay was conducted as described earlier [41,43]. Data were analyzed using FCS Express (De Novo software).

The studies were performed in triplicates, and the data are expressed as the mean ± standard deviation. The data was analyzed with Origin Pro software and GraphPad Prism version 7. The statistical significance among groups was evaluated using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). Statistical significance for group differences was established at p < 0.05 and the confidence interval was set to 95 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and size of the nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: Perylene tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), a polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon featuring an extended π-conjugated core, was explored as a precursor for carbon quantum dot (CD) synthesis. Inspired by previous work using perylenediimide and coronene, this study synthesized and characterized perylene-derived CDs. Specifically, a single-step hydrothermal method was employed to fabricate perylene-fused folic acid carbon quantum dots (Per FA CDs), and their potential as novel, tumor-targeting photosensitizers was compared to that of perylene-derived CDs (PerCDs) (Scheme 1 ).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images at different magnification scales (Fig. 1 ) showed that both Per CDs and Per FA CDs were uniformly spherical and monodispersed, with average diameters of 6.8 ± 0.7 nm and 7.4 ± 0.4 nm, respectively. The larger d-spacing in Per FA CDs compared to Per CDs suggests the successful incorporation of folic acid. The introduction of heteroatoms and functional groups from folic acid disrupts the graphitic stacking, leading to an increased interlayer distance. In contrast, CDs synthesized solely from perylene exhibit more ordered sp2 domains and thus smaller d-spacing [44].

Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was employed to determine the average hydrodynamic diameter of the carbon dots. The PerCDs exhibited a hydrodynamic diameter of 96.3 ± 8.1 nm with a polydispersity index (PDI) of 0.228. In contrast, PerFACDs displayed a significantly larger hydrodynamic diameter of 143 ± 5.3 nm with a PDI of 0.255. It is well-established that DLS measurements typically yield with larger particle sizes compared to TEM due to the measurement principle involving the hydrodynamic radius of the particles in solution [32].

Particle size is generally increased when folic acid is applied to carbon quantum dots because folic acid molecules, which are bigger and more complicated than the carbon dot molecules alone, encircle their surface [45]. Folic acid molecules on carbon quantum dot surfaces can influence particle-water interactions in other ways as well. These molecules might encase carbon quantum dots in a hydration shell, which would make them look larger in DLS measurements.

Fig. 2A compares the FTIR spectra of CDs and precursor PTCDA, showcasing distinct absorption bands. The successful solvothermal fusion of PTCDA with folic acid is evidenced by the presence of characteristic peaks. The bands detected at a wavenumber of 1600 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrational patterns of OH groups, specifically the stretching and bending motions [46]. These OH groups are found in cysteine, a component of folic acid [47]. In addition, the folic acid molecule displayed a benzoic vibration with a frequency of 1515 cm-1, illustrating the chemical interaction between PTCDA and folic acid. Alternatively, there are two distinct peaks observed at around 760 and 807 cm−1, which can be attributed to the Csp2-H bending mode of perylene [48]. The O − H peak primarily originates from the hydroxyl groups and is further confirmed by the FT-IR signal at 1240 cm−1, which is caused by the C − OH vibration [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zeta potential of the synthesized nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The surface electrical charge (zeta potential) of carbon quantum dots (CDs), colloidal nanoparticles (NPs), is crucial for assessing their stability and adherence [50]. NPs have greater stability, with zeta potential approaching zero. It's understood that surface charge not only maintains colloidal stability and enhances interactions with cells and the environment but also impacts encapsulation efficiency [51]. Qualitative analysis of the uniformity and stability of Per CDs and Per FA CDs was performed by measuring their zeta potential (Fig. 2B). The results showed that both types of CDs displayed negative surface charges at pH 7.4 (−16.3 and − 18.2 mV, respectively) and pH 5 (−9.48 and − 11.6 mV, respectively). This negativity stems from the carboxyl groups in their surface coating, leading to an anionic layer [48,52]. The observed variation in zeta potential with pH can be explained by the protonation of surface groups at low pH, resulting in a less negative charge, and deprotonation at high pH, leading to a more negative value [53]. The difference in zeta potential between Per FACDs and Per CDs confirms the successful surface modification with folic acid. Furthermore, zeta potential reflects the stability of particle suspensions, directly correlating with the surface charge of the CDs. These results indicate that both Per CDs and Per FACDs are stable and monodisperse nanoparticles [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman analysis of the synthesized carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Figs. 2(C&D) illustrate the Raman spectra and corresponding vibrational analysis of Per CD and Per FACD. For comparison, the Raman spectrum of the precursor, PTCDA was shown in Fig. S1. For Per CD, characteristic D and G bands were observed at approximately 1366 and 1566 cm−1, respectively, while Per FACD showed these bands at around 1366 and 1567 cm−1. These bands are typical of carbonaceous materials, with the D-band intensity related to the level of disordered sp3 carbon and the G-band intensity indicative of the ordered graphitic (sp2) carbon content within the synthesized carbon dots (CDs). The ratio of the intensity of the D band to that of the G band (I D/I G) indicates the graphitization degree of the synthesized samples. The I D/IG ratio for Per CD was 1.202, whereas for Per FACD it was 1.45. This indicates an increase in the I D/IG ratio, suggesting structural disorder and the introduction of additional defects or surface functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine groups [55]. A small shift in the position of the G band was observed upon the addition of folic acid, attributed to the formation of CN, CO, and C–OH bonds between perylene and folic acid [56]. This was confirmed by FTIR and XPS, as previously mentioned.

Furthermore, the Raman spectra exhibit relatively narrow D and G bands, indicative of a moderate level of structural disorder and incomplete carbonization. This observation is consistent with the retention of polymeric surface layers and residual molecular domains, a characteristic often attributed to the nature of the precursor materials and the synthesis parameters employed. This result corroborates earlier investigations utilizing folic acid as a carbon dot precursor, which similarly documented analogous structural modifications and functional group incorporation [56].

The Raman peaks observed at 1291 cm−1 (Per CDs) and 1293 cm−1 (Per FACDs) are typical of CC stretching, CH bending, or ring deformation modes within the perylene aromatic system [57], indicating in-plane vibrational modes of the aromatic rings, possibly involving the perylene core's C–C/C–H framework [57]. The shift from 1291 to 1293 cm−1 upon folic acid conjugation, which introduces functional groups like –COOH and –NH₂, implies a change in the perylene ring's electron environment. This alteration, potentially through π–π interactions or hydrogen bonding, can influence the vibrational frequencies of the molecule. Consequently, the Raman shift observed likely arises from folic acid's interaction (covalent or non-covalent) affecting the electron distribution and thus the vibrational energy levels of the conjugated perylene system [58,59].

The elemental composition and chemical bonding of the as-synthesized PerCDs, and PerFACDs were verified using XPS (Fig. 3B- H). The XPS survey spectrum for Per CD displayed peaks at 285.04, and 532.04 eV corresponding to C 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. While XPS survey spectrum for Per FA CD displayed peaks at 285.04, 532.04, and 400.83 eV corresponding to C 1 s, O 1 s, and N1s respectively, confirming that the Per FA CDs consist of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1 s, peaks at 284.4, 285.4, and 288.0 eV were observed, which are attributed to the C–C/C–H, CO /C–N, and CO groups in Per FA CDs, respectively [60]. For the O 1 s high-resolution spectrum, peaks at 531.3, 532.2, and 533.1 eV correspond to CO, OC O, and CO groups [61]. The N 1 s spectrum showed four peaks at 398.9, 399.6, 400.0, and 400.6 eV, confirming the presence of nitrogen, graphitic nitrogen, NH, and surface NH2 groups in the Per FA CDs [62].

The optical properties of the samples were analyzed using ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The UV − Vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4A) of the raw PTCDA dissolved in DMF exhibited distinct peaks at a wavelength of 410 nm [63]. The absorption spectra showed changes in both the position and shape of the peaks following the synthesis of carbon quantum dots from PTCDA. The characteristic peaks of perylene underwent a redshift to longer wavelengths (488 nm) following the formation of both PerCD and PerFACD. The absorption bands at 274 nm and 283 nm in the UV spectra for PerCDs, and PerFACD respectively, are generally ascribed to the π-π* transitions of sp2 carbon (aromatic) bonds [64]. Transitions occur when electrons in the π bonds of conjugated carbon systems are excited to higher energy levels (π* states) [65]. The absorption observed in this range is typical of materials possessing aromatic structures, including carbon quantum dots, graphene, and other carbon-based nanomaterials, which exhibit a significant degree of conjugation in their architecture [66]. The variation in absorption bands at 274 nm and 283 nm may suggest minor structural differences between the two types of carbon quantum dots PerCDs, and PerFACD, potentially arising from factors such as size, surface functionalization with folic acid, or the extent of conjugation in the carbon core [67].

The stronger absorption band at 283 nm compared to 274 nm suggests enhanced conjugation, potentially due to variations in the electronic environment or aromaticity of the carbon dot structures, and confirms successful folic acid conjugation [68,69]. These bands indicate a graphitic or aromatic core, crucial for the carbon quantum dots' electronic and optical properties [70]. The extent of conjugation within this core significantly affects their photoluminescence, while the sharpness and position of absorption bands provide insights into the size and structure of the carbon quantum dots [71].

Upon photoexcitation using 600 nm, red fluorescence emission peaked at 600 nm for PTCDA, PerCD, and PerFACD (Fig. 4B). Significantly, the synthesized carbon quantum dots showed higher fluorescence intensity than the PTCDA precursor. The enhanced emission of PerFACDs over PerCDs likely arises from their distinct precursor materials and resulting architectures. PerFACDs were synthesized using perylene anhydride and folic acid, while PerCDs were derived solely from perylene. The inclusion of folic acid in PerFACDs likely modifies their surface chemistry and functionalization, improving stability and photoluminescence efficiency [72]. This aligns with previous findings that larger conjugated systems and surface modifications, such as PVP, enhance carbon dot emission properties. The superior photoluminescence of PerFACDs probably results from the combined effects of increased conjugation and surface passivation due to folic acid. In contrast, PerCDs, lacking these benefits, exhibited lower emission intensity than PerFACDs.

Furthermore, PerFACD exhibited a nearly excitation-independent fluorescence emission, as shown in Fig. 4C and D. The synthesized carbon quantum dots emit in the red region of the visible spectrum, a significant characteristic advantageous for applications such as bioimaging, optoelectronics, and light-emitting devices [73]. The increased fluorescence intensity of the carbon quantum dots compared to PTCDA suggests that the carbonization process or the conversion to carbon quantum dots enhances the material's luminescent properties. Carbon quantum dots are known for their high quantum yields and stability, often demonstrating improved photoluminescence due to structural modifications during synthesis, including the formation of new functional groups or a more ordered carbon core. Fig. 4 (C and D) illustrates the relatively consistent fluorescence emission of the material despite variations in excitation wavelength. This excitation-independent behavior is beneficial for practical applications, indicating that PerFACDs can emit light efficiently under diverse excitation conditions, thereby increasing their versatility and reliability. This consistent emission is typically associated with carbon quantum dots possessing a uniform surface state or a well-defined core structure [74].

The fluorescence quantum yields (ΦF) of PerCD and PerFACDs were determined using a comparative method based on integrated photoluminescence fluorescence intensities. This approach involves comparing the emission intensity of the sample with that of a reference compound of known quantum yield under identical experimental conditions. The water-soluble N,N′-di(2-(trimethylammoniumiodide)ethylene) perylenediimide (TAIPDI) dye was used as the standard material. As recently reported, the fluorescence quantum yield of TAIPDI [0.05 mM] in water was found to be 0.028 [35]. Based on these considerations and the integrated fluorescence intensities of both the examined compounds and the standard TAIPDI, the fluorescence quantum yields of PerCD and PerFACDs were determined to be 0.19 and 0.22, respectively (Fig. S2). These values are consistent with those found in the literature for the reported carbon dots [75,76]. Such considerably high fluorescence quantum yields of water-soluble carbon quantum dots exhibited strong red fluorescence, suggesting promising potential for bioimaging applications in aqueous environments.

Fig. S3 depicts the photoluminescence intensity of the synthesized per CD and per FACD in the presence of serum, a biological fluid, and PBs that simulate biological fluid. It was found that in the case of per CD, there is a reduction in photoluminescence intensity in the blood sample, whereas the presence of folic acid with per FACD results in an increase in photoluminescence intensity. Serum is a multifaceted biological medium consisting of proteins (such as albumin), amino acids, salts, lipids, and enzymes [77]. Quenching effects in serum may arise from the transfer of energy and electrons between carbon dots and serum proteins [78], static quenching due to protein attachment to carbon dots [79], or dynamic quenching through collisional interactions [80]. The incorporation of folic acid modifies surface chemistry, resulting in enhanced surface passivation. Folic acid functions as a passivating agent and reduces non-radiative recombination. Prevent adverse interactions between the CD surface and serum proteins [81].

Perylene anhydride's propensity for oxidative coupling and π–π stacking under hydrothermal/solvothermal conditions likely facilitates polymeric/graphitic domain formation within the carbon dot core [84]. These structures may lead to delocalized electronic states, resulting in the observed red-shifted and excitation-independent emission. These findings correspond with prior literature indicating that perylene-based carbon dots have dual-emission characteristics owing to the presence of surface states and polymeric inside structures [85]. Furthermore, perylene contributes to a polymeric surface network integrated into the core during synthesis, enhancing dual fluorescence and reinforcing the carbonaceous nature of the core [76].

Fig. 5
. (A) and (C) PL-excitation contour maps of Per CDs in aqueous solutions at room temperature, (B) and (D) PL-excitation contour maps of PerFACDs in aqueous solutions at room temperature.

The dual emission observed in both core and surface regions indicates stable fluorescence, a beneficial property for precise monitoring and effective light absorption in photodynamic therapy (PDT), as stable emission spectra are essential for precise monitoring of therapeutic progress and for ensuring effective light absorption for reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation [86]. Furthermore, the uniformity in the emission maxima implies that both regions may contribute comparably to ROS production, albeit with varying efficiencies contingent upon the excitation wavelengths employed. In addition, the capability to excite the synthesized CDs at two different wavelengths may prove advantageous for PDT, as it offers versatility in selecting a suitable light source for activation. Adjusting the excitation wavelength could potentially enhance the production of ROS in various tissue environments, taking into account the light's penetration depth and the unique characteristics of the synthesized CDs [87].

Hemolysis and MTT assays of normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells were performed to evaluate the biocompatibility and biosafety of synthesized Per CD and Per FA CD. As depicted in Fig. 6
A and B, hemolysis rates for both materials remained below 2 % up to a concentration of 50 μg/ml. A haemolysis rate below 5 % is generally classified as within the normal, non-toxic range, signifying low cytotoxicity and favorable biocompatibility. A hemolysis rate exceeding 5 % serves as a potential indicator of cytotoxicity or hemolytic activity, implying that concentrations of Per CD and Per FA CD up to 50 μg/mL are likely safe for biological applications [92]. At higher Per CD concentrations, slight red coloration was observed, which is likely due to minor red blood cell damage. This damage leads to the rupture of the cells and the subsequent release of hemoglobin, causing the sample solution to turn red. In contrast, Per FA CD exhibited a lower hemolysis percentage, suggesting that folic acid incorporation into the PTCDA-derived carbon quantum dots enhances their biocompatibility and safety [93]. Fig. 6B presents MTT assay results on normal peripheral blood mononuclear cells after 48 h of incubation with varying concentrations (0–100 μg/ml) of carbon quantum dots. The results indicate non-significant toxicity at lower concentrations (6.25–50 μg/ml), whereas at a concentration of 100 μg/ml, cell viability falls below 70 %.

The assessment of carbon dot cytotoxicity on peripheral blood cells is a crucial step in validating their safety for biomedical applications, particularly for intravenous delivery systems and photodynamic therapy (PDT). In this study, both Per CDs, and Per FACDs demonstrated no significant hemolytic activity, indicating excellent hemocompatibility. These findings are especially important given the growing interest in using blood components as natural carriers for nanotherapeutics, including for cancer-targeted PDT.

Peripheral blood cells such as erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets play critical roles in maintaining homeostasis and immune surveillance, which are the first biological interface encountered following intravenous administration [94]. Any nanoparticle intended for systemic therapy must not induce hemolysis, coagulation, or immune activation. The absence of hemolytic activity by both Per CDs, and Per FACDs suggests that these materials do not disrupt erythrocyte membranes, a common challenge with other photoactive or aromatic-based nanomaterials. This is particularly promising for their use in PDT, where carbon dots may be delivered through the bloodstream to tumor tissues, either passively via the enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect or actively via targeting ligands like folic acid [95]. Furthermore, the folic acid functionalization in Per FACDs may confer additional benefits beyond targeting. FA is known to improve colloidal stability in biological fluids and reduce nonspecific interactions with plasma proteins and blood cell membranes. This could explain the slightly higher biocompatibility observed with Per FACDs compared to unmodified PCDs [32].

Furthermore, the cytotoxicity of perylene carbon dots (Per CDs) and perylene-folic acid carbon dots (Per FACDs) was assessed using the MTT assay on normal human skin fibroblast normal cell line (HFF-1) HF1 cells Fig. 6 D. The results demonstrated excellent biocompatibility for both types of carbon dots, as indicated by their CC50 values being greater than 100 μg/ml (CC50 for Per CD, and Per FACDs were 118.38 and 236.21 μg/ml). This suggests that neither formulation exerts significant cytotoxic effects on normal cells at commonly used concentrations, making them promising candidates for PDT applications. The conjugation of folic acid to perylene to produce Per FACDs did not increase cytotoxicity, which is significant given that surface modifications can often alter the interaction of nanomaterials with biological systems. Folic acid is known for its role in targeting folate receptors, which are overexpressed in many cancer cells, and its addition here may potentially improve selective uptake in cancer cells without harming normal tissues [96].

When comparing these results to other reported carbon dot systems, our findings are consistent with the general trend of low toxicity for carbon-based nanomaterials. For example, Havrdova et al. (2016) reported that graphene quantum dots exhibited CC₅₀ values >100 μg/mL on various cell lines [97], while Wang et al. (2014) found that nitrogen-doped carbon dots maintained over 90 % cell viability at concentrations up to 100 μg/ml [98]. Similarly, Qu et al. (2015) showed that polyethylene glycol (PEG)-coated carbon dots had minimal cytotoxicity, with CC₅₀ values often exceeding 150 μg/ml [99]. Compared to these, both PCDs and PFA-CDs demonstrate comparable or slightly better biocompatibility, supporting their safe application in biomedical settings.

The dark toxicity assessment revealed a similar finding to the one obtained from the hemolysis experiment (Fig. 7 A) and the safety assessment tested on mononuclear cells, and HFF-1 cell lines (Fig. 6 C, and D). As shown in Fig. 7 A and B, PC-3 cells tolerated PerFACD significantly more than PerCDs. The viability of the cells incubated with 50 μg/mL of PerFACD was almost 100 % in dark condition, while the viability of the cells incubated with the same concentration and dark condition from PerCDs was 80 %. To assess the differential cytotoxicity of the synthesized carbon dots between normal and cancerous cells, the selectivity index (SI) was calculated for PerCDs and PerFACD under dark conditions. The SI, defined as the ratio of the CC₅₀ value in normal human fibroblast cells (HF1) to that in prostate cancer cells (PC3), was found to be 1.47 for PerCDs and 2.4 for PerFACD. These results indicate that PerFACD exhibit significantly greater selectivity toward cancer cells compared to Per CDs. The enhanced selectivity is attributed to the presence of folic acid, which facilitates targeted uptake via folate receptor-mediated endocytosis receptors that are overexpressed in PC3 cells but minimally present in HF1 cells [100].

This finding further strengthens that folic acid conjugation positively impacted the safety profile of the NPs. Otherwise, pervious study demonstrated selective uptake of FA-conjugated carbon dots in folate receptor-positive HeLa cells over NIH-3 T3 normal cells, emphasizing the targeting capability of FA-conjugated CDs [101]. In addation, pervious work demonstrated selective uptake of folic acid-conjugated carbon dots in cancer cells (HepG2) over normal cells (PC-12), suggesting potential for targeted imaging applications [102].

Based on the obtained UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 4A), the wavelength selected for illumination was 520 nm (Fig. S3). Two irradiation times were tested, and it was observed that the longer the irradiation time was, the stronger the cytotoxic effect was for a given concentration of the NPs. After 30 min of irradiation, cell viability dropped to 70 % and 45 % when 25 μg/mL of plain CDs (Per CD) and folic acid-conjugated CDs (PerFACD) were incubated, respectively. Extending irradiation to 60 min while maintaining the concentration significantly reduced viability to 30 % and 7 % for Per CD and Per FACD, respectively. The control group exposed solely to 520 nm laser radiation (1.5 W/cm2) showed no significant cytotoxicity (Fig. S5). This finding confirmed that CDs effectively eliminated cancer cells upon laser irradiation and can be explained by the production of ROS, as indicated by the measured singlet oxygen quantum yield.

Importantly, Per FA CD exhibited a significantly enhanced PDT capability (p < 0.05) compared to Per CD, likely due to the targeted delivery facilitated by folic acid conjugation. Although the cellular uptake was not quantified in this study, previous studies have shown that nanoparticles (NPs) with high affinity for target molecules can effectively deliver therapeutic agents to prostate cancer compared to the untargeted ones [51].

The synthesized PerFACD demonstrates significant potential for rapid penetration into PC-3 cancer cells, likely due to its small size and folic acid incorporation [62]. This is consistent with previous research utilizing folic acid-modified carbon quantum dots for tumor targeting in folate receptor-positive cancers and bioimaging of HeLa and A549 cells [103]. Furthermore, hydrothermally produced carbon quantum dots from folic acid have shown notable therapeutic efficacy in doxorubicin delivery [104]. Our prior work also employed folic acid carbon quantum dots to target curcumin to HepG2 cells [105].

Based on MTT assay results (Fig. 7B), cells were treated with 12.5 μg/mL PerFACD, the lowest concentration inducing over 50 % cell death. Flow cytometry analysis (Fig. 8A-C) showed no significant cell death in the normal, dark, or light controls, with living cell populations exceeding 92 %. However, 30-min irradiation significantly increased early and late apoptotic, as well as necrotic subpopulations, compared to the control (Fig. 7D). Extending irradiation to 60 min resulted in a substantial increase in necrosis (nearly 50 %) and a corresponding decrease in living cells (to 40 %), with minimal apoptosis (Fig. 7E). These flow cytometry findings corroborate the MTT assay results (Fig. 6B), highlighting the crucial role of irradiation duration in PerFACD's cytotoxic effect.

The mechanism of cell death apoptotic, or necrotic is determined by several factors, including the degree of cellular damage, the nature of stimuli (e.g., radiation, chemical agents), and the intrinsic signaling pathways triggered within the cell. Apoptosis is generally marked by caspase activation, DNA fragmentation, and membrane blebbing, while necrosis is linked to cell membrane rupture, inflammatory responses, and the release of intracellular contents [106]. Research indicates that extended or intensified stressors, such as prolonged irradiation durations, can alter the equilibrium from apoptosis to necrosis, as necrosis often arises when damage exceeds the cell's reparative capacity [107]. Furthermore, the microenvironment, encompassing elements such as oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction, has been associated with influencing the mechanism of cell death in reaction to diverse stimuli [108]. These investigations corroborate our findings that extended irradiation durations result in heightened necrosis, hence underscoring the significance of irradiation time in influencing the cytotoxic effect.

A direct, one-step hydrothermal approach was developed for synthesizing water-soluble perylene tetracarboxylic acid carbon quantum dots and their folic acid coated counterparts. Notably, this synthesis exclusively utilizes water as the solvent, eliminating the need for hazardous chemicals or catalysts. The resulting carbon quantum dots exhibit exceptional promise as photosensitizers for cancer treatment, particularly within the context of photodynamic therapy. Their unique amalgamation of enhanced photostability, intense fluorescence, and inherent biocompatibility makes them particularly attractive. Per FACD demonstrated greater safety for normal cells, as cytotoxicity assays using human fibroblast cells (HF1) and peripheral blood cells showed no significant toxicity for Per FACD. To evaluate their selectivity toward cancer cells, the selectivity index (SI) was calculated based on cytotoxicity in PC3 prostate cancer cells versus normal cells under dark conditions. The selectivity index was 1.47 for Per CDs and 2.4 for Per FACD when cancerous cell line PC-3 was compared to the normal one HFF-1 demonstrating the importance of functionalization in promoting selective uptake and thus subsequent lethality. These findings highlight the potential of folic acid-functionalized carbon dots for targeted cancer therapy with minimal effects on healthy cells. In vitro PDT studies demonstrated significant inhibition of prostate cancer cells (PC3), with cell viability markedly reduced to 7 % upon exposure to light radiation in the presence of Per-FACD. The induced cell death mode was found to be necrotic at longer durations. The study encourages further investigations to better understand the anticancer response and evaluated it at the in vivo level.
